worth_it_explanation: Names and codes are the keys every downstream task reads, so a drifting convention raises the cost of targeting, reporting, and handoffs across the whole network. The check is fast and visual. You scan the ad unit tree and a sample of orders and line items in Google Ad Manager (GAM) for a pattern, and the absence of one is obvious. Fixing the convention is mostly documentation plus renaming, not a rebuild, and it pays back on every report filter and every new trafficker who has to learn the inventory.
intro: Consistent names and codes for ad units, orders, and line items are what make targeting reliable and reporting filterable. When the convention drifts, key-value targeting becomes error-prone, report filters stop catching everything they should, and onboarding a new trafficker takes longer. These checks confirm that a documented naming convention exists and that the inventory tree and the delivery records actually follow it.

audit_checks.title: Ad unit hierarchy and codes are inconsistent
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Ad unit names and codes that drift in casing, abbreviation, or hierarchy make key-value targeting and report filters error-prone, and they slow down every trafficker who has to learn the inventory by reading inconsistent labels rather than a pattern.

educate.base:
In Google Ad Manager (GAM), inventory is organized as a tree of ad units, where each ad unit represents an ad slot and child ad units inherit settings from their parents. Each ad unit carries a name and a code. The name is the human-readable label traffickers read in the tree, and the code is the identifier the ad tag uses on the page. Both are referenced constantly, in targeting, in reporting filters, and in the tags developers place, so the words and structure you choose become the keys the rest of the setup depends on.

When the hierarchy and codes drift, the inventory stops reading as a pattern. One ad unit is named 'Homepage_Top', a sibling is 'home-leaderboard', and a third buries the position in an abbreviation only the person who created it understands. The cost shows up downstream. A trafficker filtering a report by ad unit has to know every spelling variant to catch them all, and an ad unit named in an unexpected way slips past the filter and out of the analysis. Targeting a group of slots becomes manual rather than predictable.

A convention fixes this by deciding, up front, what each level of the tree represents and how a name encodes it. A common approach orders the parts from general to specific, for example placement or section, then size, then position. Applied top-down from the network's root, the convention makes a name self-describing and a filter reliable because the same concept is always spelled the same way.

A few signs point to an inconsistent ad unit hierarchy:
  • Mixed casing or separators across sibling ad units, such as 'Homepage_Top' next to 'home-leaderboard'
  • Ad-hoc abbreviations that are not documented anywhere, so only the author can decode them
  • A flat tree where every slot sits at the root, or a deep tree where levels do not map to a consistent meaning
  • Codes that do not follow any visible scheme, which makes the on-page tags hard to audit
You may want to compare the tree against a written convention. If no convention is documented, that absence is itself the finding because the consistency you see today depends on whoever happened to create each ad unit.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media property, the inventory tree usually mirrors the site's sections and ad positions, so the convention is most useful when section, size, and position are each encoded in a predictable order. You may want to confirm that new sections added over time still follow the original scheme rather than introducing a parallel naming style.

learn_more.source_title: Planning the structure of your inventory
Create or configure an ad unit

investigate.intro: Review the ad unit tree and the codes in Google Ad Manager for a consistent, documented pattern across names, casing, hierarchy, and codes.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Inventory', then 'Ad units', and expand the tree from the network's root down through the child ad units.
Scan the names across siblings and levels. Note whether casing, separators, and abbreviations follow one pattern or vary from unit to unit.
Open a few ad units and read the 'Code' field. Note whether the codes follow a visible scheme that maps to the names, or whether they look ad-hoc.
Check whether a naming convention is documented anywhere the team can reference, for example an internal trafficking guide. Record whether one exists and whether the tree matches it.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a documented naming convention exists, and whether the ad unit names, casing, hierarchy, and codes follow it across a representative sample of the tree. A tree that follows a documented convention consistently passes. A tree with mixed casing, undocumented abbreviations, an inconsistent hierarchy, or codes that follow no visible scheme is a fail, recorded with examples of the inconsistent ad units.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether a naming convention is documented and whether the ad unit tree and codes follow it above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the ad unit hierarchy and codes on {{site_name}} do not follow a consistent, documented convention. Names drift in casing and abbreviation across sibling ad units, which makes key-value targeting error-prone and lets ad units slip past report filters that do not anticipate every spelling variant. The inconsistency also slows onboarding since a new trafficker has to learn the inventory unit by unit rather than from a pattern. {{voice_recommend}} defining a naming convention that encodes placement, size, and position in a predictable order, documenting it, and applying it top-down from the network root so names are self-describing and filters are reliable. After the change, spot-check a sample of ad units and their codes against the documented convention.

audit_checks.title: Orders and line items lack a consistent naming structure
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Unstructured order and line-item names break report filters, make competitive-exclusion and priority management harder to reason about, and complicate handoffs between sales and trafficking since no one can tell what a line item is from its name alone.

educate.base:
In Google Ad Manager (GAM), an order groups the line items that deliver a single advertiser's campaign, and each line item carries its own targeting, priority, and pricing. Orders and line items are named when they are created, and those names are what traffickers, sales, and analysts read in the 'Delivery' section and in every report that breaks results out by order or line item. The name is the only context most people get at a glance, so an unstructured name forces everyone to open the record to learn what it actually is.

When order and line-item names follow no structure, several tasks get harder at once. Report filters that rely on a name pattern, for example pulling every line item for one advertiser or one campaign type, miss records that were named differently. Reasoning about competitive exclusion and priority becomes slower because a trafficker cannot tell a high-priority sponsorship from a low-priority remnant line item without opening each one. Handoffs between sales and trafficking lose information since the person picking up a campaign has only an ambiguous label to work from.

A naming structure solves this by encoding the same facts in the same order every time. A common structure for an order or line item includes the advertiser, the campaign, the line item type, and the flight dates, so the name itself answers who the campaign is for, what it is, how it competes, and when it runs. The exact fields matter less than the consistency. A documented structure that everyone follows is what makes the names filterable and the handoffs clean.

A few signs point to unstructured order and line-item names:
  • Names that vary in which facts they include, so one line item carries the flight dates and another does not
  • Free-text labels with no advertiser, campaign, or type encoded, which forces a trafficker to open the record to understand it
  • Report filters by name that return incomplete results because the naming is not consistent enough to match on
You may want to check whether a documented structure exists and whether recent orders and line items follow it. As with ad units, an undocumented convention drifts when different people create records their own way.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media property running both direct-sold sponsorships and programmatic demand, a clear naming structure helps separate the two at a glance and makes priority and competitive-exclusion decisions easier to audit. You may want to confirm that direct-sold orders and house or remnant line items follow the same documented structure rather than each team naming records its own way.

investigate.intro: Scan a sample of orders and line items in Google Ad Manager for a consistent, documented naming scheme that encodes the advertiser, campaign, type, and flight.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Delivery', then 'Orders', and review the list of recent orders. Note whether the names follow one structure or read as free text.
Open a few orders and review their line items. Note whether the line-item names encode the same facts in the same order, for example advertiser, campaign, type, and flight dates.
Try filtering the orders or line items list by a name pattern, for example one advertiser. Note whether the filter returns every record you expect or misses some because they were named differently.
Check whether a naming structure is documented for the team to follow. Record whether one exists and whether the recent orders and line items match it.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a documented naming structure exists for orders and line items, and whether a representative sample of recent records follows it. Orders and line items that follow a documented, filterable structure pass. Free-text or inconsistent names that omit the advertiser, campaign, type, or flight, or that cause a name-based filter to return incomplete results, are a fail, recorded with examples.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether an order and line-item naming structure is documented and whether recent records follow it above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that orders and line items on {{site_name}} do not follow a consistent naming structure. The names vary in which facts they encode, so a trafficker cannot tell what a line item is, how it competes, or when it runs without opening the record, and report filters that match on a name pattern miss records that were named differently. The inconsistency also makes priority and competitive-exclusion decisions slower and complicates handoffs between sales and trafficking. {{voice_recommend}} defining a naming structure that encodes the advertiser, campaign, line item type, and flight in a fixed order, documenting it, and applying it to new orders and line items so the records are filterable and self-describing. After the change, spot-check recent orders and line items against the documented structure.
